summary: Python scripts are .py files executed with 'python3 filename.py'. Python compiles source to bytecode (.pyc in __pycache__) for faster loading. sys.argv contains command-line arguments. The REPL (python3 with no arguments) runs interactive Python sessions. __name__ == '__main__' is the guard that separates runnable script code from importable module code.
oneLineSummary: Python reads your .py file top-to-bottom, compiles to bytecode, and executes it; use __name__ == '__main__' to protect entry-point code.
keyTakeaways: sys.argv is a list where [0]=script name, [1+]=arguments
REPL auto-prints expressions; scripts need explicit print()
__name__ == '__main__' only True when run directly, not when imported
Python caches bytecode in __pycache__/*.pyc for performance
Never name scripts after standard library modules (os.py, math.py)

memoryTricks.trick: Think: 'Am I the main character or a supporting character?' If you're run directly, you're the main character (__name__ == '__main__'). If you're imported, you play a supporting role.

commonErrors.cause: Named your script the same as a standard library module (e.g., random.py)
commonErrors.fix: Rename your script to something unique

commonErrors.error: IndexError: list index out of range on sys.argv[1]
commonErrors.cause: Accessing sys.argv[1] but no arguments were passed

preInterviewChecklist: Explain the REPL vs script differences

nextTopics.title: Variables & Data Types
nextTopics.whyNext: With Python running, learn the fundamental building blocks: variables and the types of data Python can store.
